projects.title: TOPOGRAPHY OF AFTERIMAGE
projects.blurb: A RING OF LIGHT, PAIRED WITH LOW FREQUENCY SOUND, WORKS LIKE A LENS ON THE BUNKER WALL.
description:
MADE DURING ARTEZ' DAT PROGRAMS' PROJECT WEEKS UNDER THE THEME 'TRANSMISSIONS'.


A RING OF LIGHT, PAIRED WITH LOW FREQUENCY SOUND, WORKS LIKE A LENS ON THE BUNKER WALL.


IN COLLABORATION WITH LINA PTUSHKINA AND KEE PALENQUE LOBATO, WE SPENT TIME IN THE DIOGENES BUNKER IN ARNHEM, ABSORBING ITS CHARACTERISTICS, HISTORY, AND THE FEELINGS THAT AROSE. 

EXPLORING THE THEME OF 'TRANSMISSIONS', WE STARTED FROM A SMALL EXPERIMENT: POINTING A PHONE LIGHT AT A WALL THAT HAD BEEN HIT BY SHRAPNEL. MOVEMENT OF THIS LIGHT REVEALED LAYERS OF CONSTRUCTION AND DECONSTRUCTION, CRACKS AND REPAIRS, STAYED AS AFTERIMAGES. 

THEY MADE US RAISE QUESTIONS ABOUT WHERE THESE MARKS COME FROM. WITH TIME WE GREW MORE SENSITIVE TO THE SITE AS BOTH WITNESS AND ARCHIVE OF EVENTS AND LIVES WE COULD HARDLY IMAGINE. 

OUR WORK RIFFS ON THAT SMALL EXPERIMENT AND OUR TIME IN THE BUNKER, USING CHOREOGRAPHED LIGHT THAT ECHOES SIGNALS ONCE USED IN WAR AND BRINGS THE SURFACE INTO MOTION. IT HIGHLIGHTS THE DYNAMIC INTERPLAY OF MOMENTS OF CONSTRUCTION AND DECONSTRUCTION AND CREATES ERRATIC PATTERNS OF CRACKS AND REPAIRS THAT MAKE US SPECULATE ON THE IMPACT OF BOMBS, OR THE ATTEMPTS TO (RE)BUILD AND CONCEAL.


MATERIALS: WOODEN REBUILD OF A WALL INSIDE DIOGENES BUNKER, LED RING, SOUND

EXHIBITED AT 'EMERGING EXITS', ARNHEM (NL)

projects.title: Digital Window
projects.blurb: A rolling window frame breathes ultrasonic mist at whoever comes close — an operating system rendered as weather.
description: A 1.20 m × 95 cm frame styled after a desktop window, mounted on a rolling steel trolley. Ultrasonic mist fills the frame and reacts to viewer proximity: the closer you come, the more the “window” exhales.

Driven by an ESP32 running a state-machine interaction model with a WiFi manual-override mode, MOSFET-switched fans and mist generators. The piece treats the interface metaphor literally — a window that has weather instead of content.

media.caption: Mist reaction test — paste a direct .mp4 link

projects.title: Living Monolith
projects.blurb: Hand-wound electromagnets breathe beneath a skin of iron filings, making an invisible field momentarily visible.
description: A sculpture built around hand-wound coils on a mild-steel core. Iron filings on its surface stand up, shiver and collapse as the electromagnetic field below them breathes — slow cycles interrupted by sudden freezes.

The work began as a ferrofluid study and pivoted to dry filings after long material research; the result is harsher and more mineral, closer to a geological process than a fluid one. States are switchable over WiFi.

projects.title: Glitch Explorer
projects.blurb: Three performers browse the web while a hacked extension turns the colors under their cursors into a synchronized score.
description: A Chrome extension that turns ordinary browsing into ensemble performance. As three performers move their cursors across web pages, scanned pixel colors drive a shared six-instrument sound engine over the network.
The sound world borrows from Ryoji Ikeda — sine bursts, clicks, data noise — so that the visual texture of the everyday web is heard as signal traffic.

media.caption: Live set excerpt — paste Vimeo ID

projects.title: Reading the Lots, Hearing the Horse
projects.blurb: An artistic research project treating pre-industrial divination as a serious collective decision technology.
description: A research article and pair of A2 posters examining how pre-industrial communities outsourced hard decisions to procedures — cast lots, horse omens, river voices — and what these systems share with contemporary decision infrastructure.
The text moves from Germanic lot-casting through the legal personhood of the Whanganui River to Andean ayllu practice, set in a punk-zine print language.

projects.title: Saba CVC 75 N, Reborn
projects.blurb: A 1980s tube camera converted into a tapeless digital rig — Newvicon ghosting and all.
description: A vintage Saba CVC 75 N video camera (a rebadged Hitachi with a Newvicon pickup tube) converted to record without its long-dead cassette deck: a mini DVR and 12 V battery pack replace the original recorder via the 10-pin EIAJ connector.
The tube’s smearing, blooming and comet-tail highlights become the material of ongoing video work — an analog sensor misbehaving beautifully inside a digital pipeline.
